title: An agent can follow your runbook, but it can't inherit the consequences
summary: Addy Osmani argues the durable engineering job is owning the verdict — agents run the inner loop, but deciding, verifying, and accepting risk stay with a human who can be answerable.
takeaways: Split the loop: let agents run the inner loop — investigate, implement, test, report — but keep the outer loop, decide and verify and approve and own, with a human who can be answerable.
Every engineering edge decays about one model release at a time — speed, recall, verification, even taste — so the durable role isn't a capability, it's owning the verdict.
Make the rule 'explain it or don't ship it': someone has to understand the work well enough to defend it, because the agent can accept no risk and owns no blast radius.
sourceName: The engineer of the future — Addy Osmani, AI Engineer (2026)

sources.title: The engineer of the future — Addy Osmani, AI Engineer (2026)

body:
## What happened

In a [2026-07-14 AI Engineer talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n97BCfyFIvw), Addy Osmani argued that the engineer of the future is "the person who is able to choose what is worth doing" and owns the verdict — the production decision to ship, block, redirect, or accept risk. His boundary: agents run the inner loop (investigate, implement, test, report); the outer loop — "deciding, verifying, approving, owning" — stays human. "The boundary is not human looks at AI output. The boundary is evidence and responsibility."

## Why it matters

"Making generation cheaper does not automatically make review cheaper." He cites a 2026 Sonar survey: about 96% of engineers don't fully trust AI code, yet only about half always verify before committing — "distrust without bandwidth." As AI-assisted code becomes normal code, answerability stops being philosophical and becomes an engineering requirement.

## How it works

1. **Execution isn't responsibility.** "The agent can follow your runbook, but it can't inherit the consequences." When something fails, the questions are: who understood the policy, who accepted the risk, and who owns the blast radius?
2. **Your edges decay; your signature doesn't.** Speed, recall, and verification have already moved into harnesses and evals, and even taste "resets as models learn." The half-life of an edge is about one model release; the half-life of your signature — the name on the work — is far longer.
3. **Explain it or don't ship it.** Not that a human types every line, but that someone understands the change well enough to defend it — an owners-file discipline for agent-shipped code.

> Agents execute; only a human can be answerable — and as capabilities decay, that ownership is the durable job.

## The catch

Osmani's own warnings cut against easy answers: he names "cognitive surrender" (citing a Wharton study where, when AI was wrong, 73% still picked the wrong answer and felt more sure) and "orchestration tax" — your cognitive bandwidth doesn't parallelize, so more agents isn't more of you. "Own the verdict" only holds if you resist borrowed confidence; the failure mode isn't using AI, it's deferring to it before forming a view.

[Harness Engineering](/guide/harness-engineering/)
